perturbation.original_text: ii. Payment to CSA no later than thirty (30) days after receipt of invoice from CSA. CSA shall submit invoice to the Company no later than thirty (30) days after Company provides CSA with the list of the sales of Product (containing the Property with such supporting information that may be required or requested to be disclosed).
perturbation.changed_text: ii. Payment to CSA no later than sixty (60) days after receipt of invoice from CSA. CSA shall submit invoice to the Company no later than sixty (60) days after Company provides CSA with the list of the sales of Product (containing the Property with such supporting information that may be required or requested to be disclosed).
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the payment to 60 days after the receipt of the invoice could be an issue under UK law if it is deemed an unfair payment term to the supplier, especially if the contract is classified as one between a larger and smaller business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Late Payment of Commercial Debts (Interest) Act 1998 (UK)
perturbation.law_citation: Late Payment of Commercial Debts (Interest) Act 1998
perturbation.law_explanation: The Late Payment of Commercial Debts (Interest) Act 1998 implies a term into commercial contracts that allows businesses to charge interest on late payments. While the parties can agree on payment terms, these must not be grossly unfair to the supplier. Extending payment to 60 days without reasonable justification could be viewed as unfair, potentially violating the spirit, if not the precise letter, of the law regarding fair payment terms, especially considering potential power imbalances between the parties.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment terms from 30 to 60 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that this delay could be problematic under UK law if deemed unfair to the supplier, especially between larger and smaller businesses. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that while parties can agree on terms, they must not be grossly unfair to the supplier. Extending the payment to 60 days without justification could violate the spirit of the law regarding fair payment terms, especially considering potential power imbalances. The scraped law snippet mentions the Late Payment of Commercial Debts (Interest) Act 1998, indicating legislation related to late payments. This supports the assertion that the extended payment term introduces a legal contradiction by potentially violating fair payment standards as seen in 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction', where a deadline violates federal or state law.

perturbation.original_text: Talent shall have seven (7) days following receipt of such Materials or any part thereof displaying the intended use of the Property to review and for CSA to provide to Company written approval for such use.
perturbation.changed_text: Talent shall have two (2) days following receipt of such Materials or any part thereof displaying the intended use of the Property to review and for CSA to provide to Company written approval for such use.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the review period to two days for materials could be problematic under general contract law principles if it's deemed insufficient time for a reasonable review, especially if the materials are complex. This creates a legal contradiction because it undermines the Talent's right to properly review the materials before approval.
perturbation.law_explanation: General contract law requires terms to be reasonable. Reducing the review period to two days might be deemed unreasonable if the complexity of the materials requires more time for proper consideration. This could render the approval clause unenforceable, contradicting the contract's intent to provide Talent with approval rights.
perturbation.justification: The change from seven to two days for review directly contradicts the general requirement for reasonableness in contract law. While no specific statute dictates the exact timeframe for review periods, reducing it to two days, as highlighted in the Perturbation and Law Explanations, could be deemed unreasonable, particularly if the materials are complex. This undermines the Talent's ability to provide informed consent, which is a basic element of contract law. Even without explicit law snippets, the principle of 'reasonable time' implied in contractual obligations is violated, creating a legally contestable situation, as the Talent's approval right is compromised.

perturbation.original_text: Talent agrees that Company shall, for a period of nine (9) months (Sell-Off Period) following the effective date of termination, have the right to continue to sell Product bearing the Property and/or utilize advertising materials and collateral bearing the Property.
perturbation.changed_text: Talent agrees that Company shall, for a period of eighteen (18) months (Sell-Off Period) following the effective date of termination, have the right to continue to sell Product bearing the Property and/or utilize advertising materials and collateral bearing the Property.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the sell-off period to 18 months after termination could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under UK law, especially if it significantly limits the Talent's ability to exploit their own image and brand after the agreement ends.
perturbation.law_explanation: The doctrine of restraint of trade in UK law prevents agreements that unduly restrict a person's ability to trade or practice their profession. While sell-off periods are generally acceptable, extending it to 18 months could be deemed unreasonable, particularly if it prevents Talent from working with competitors or exploiting their image for a substantial period after the agreement terminates. This could render the clause unenforceable as an unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the 'Sell-Off Period' to 18 months, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under UK law. The scraped snippet from Wex defines 'restraint of trade' as any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. Extending the sell-off period could unduly restrict the Talent's ability to exploit their image and brand, thus creating a contradiction.
